raw_text: Christine Perkell. "Vergil Reading His Twentieth-Century Readers: A Study of Eclogue 9." Vergilius 47 (2001) 64-88. The "competitive" aspect of Bucolic songs, Theocritus through Vergil to the Middle Ages remains constant. Baumbach accents two different approaches to the same topic in the Fifth Bucolic, and two concepts of poetry, oral and written, with a discussion of inspiration and imitation, and the reader as judge of their merits. Breed studies song and voice in the Sixth Bucolic as an ecphrastic subject. The centerpiece (31-86) is not a dramatization but a narration by the primary narrator, Tityrus. Paschalis re-awakens interest in the interplay of science and myth in Silenus' song in the same poem. Perkell surveys the late 20th-century critical landscape (with an appended bibliography, 86-88) and finds that Eclogue 9 typifies a dichotomy of interpretive opinion divided between "optimistic" and "pessimistic" parties. The division "seems to be a function of parallel deployment of describable interpretive conventions, corresponding to observed structural features of the text." (85). Vergilian Bibliography 2001-2002 135

raw_text: Peter Toohey. Epic Lessons. An Introduction to Ancient Didactic Poetry. London and New York. 1996. Rev: Parroni, Gnomon 74 (2002) 440-42. Cramer's analysis and provocative discussion continues to challenge the 'Harvard School.' proponents of disillusionment and contrary political sentiments. Monica Gale's work calls for attentive reading and speculation on didactic poetry. Gowers is concerned with kitchen gardens and their produce in Vergil and Columella, a seductive genre which Vergil felt obliged to omit while extolling the aged Tarentine gardener. Thomas shows that English and 'colonial' responses to the Georgics lack uniformity. He scrutinizes twentiethcentury translations and adaptations of the poems, and adduces literal and cultural intertexts extending from the distant period of the English civil war to recent Irish poets, Kavanaugh and Heaney, measuring 'resistance' poets against readings of the Georgics as idealized country life.

raw_text: C. Walde. Die Traumdarstellungen in der griechischrömischen Dichtung. Munich and Leipzig. 2001. Armstrong studies the symbolic function of Crete in its repeated instances as a reminder of the temptations and inevitability of regression, repetition, and diversion from the linear course of history. Crete is portrayed as a cradle of deceit and monstrous passions, and of plague; but it is also a source of cures. Dreams, as extensions of poetic vision, images of prophecy and human psychology, and insanity are much in vogue for several scholars (Bouquet, Hershkowitz, Lake, Scafoglio, and Walde) and their studies enrich our understanding and appreciation of the poet's genius. Julia Dyson's study of Diana Nemorensis accents her importance as agent and cultic backdrop to sacrificial aspects of the epic. Lowrie's contribution to the University of Pennsylvania Conference (#5) argues for 'seriously playful' readings of Vergil's works as antidote to political and aesthetic concentration on the stark contrasts between good and evil. Markus studies Vergilian 'performance' in the context of his age (pp. 168-71). Battle narratives and casualty lists preoccupy Mazzochini. His chief concern is with poetic composition and narrative skill; his line by line commentaries on each selection is highly instructive. Most's contribution to "The Vergilian Century" (#5) examines Vergil's complete repertoire to develop new insights into memory's role in its poetic, erotic, didactic, and political dimensions. The relationship of remembering and forgetting emerges as central to major issues in Vergilian criticism. Aeneas' final act is not rooted exclusively in anger but is a gesture fuelled by memory and forgetfulness. Newman's brief essay explores neglected aspects of the Etruscan content of the epic and Vergil's possible Etruscan lineage, a favorable factor in his relationship with Etruscan Maecenas. Vergilian Bibliography 2001-2002 1 39 Oliensis examines the unexplored relationship between Vergil's ep and its place as Freudian dream work. Bloom, Hardie, and Reckfor pass in review with other neglected experimental readings of the 6 and 70s, and recent gender-oriented readings. Performance an gesture are treated from opposite poles by Campbell and Ricottill Shiffman's Platonic reading of the epic is fresh and illuminating. AENEID: INDIVIDUAL BOOKS l-VI

raw_text: Gregory A. Staley. "Vergil's Daedalus." CO 79 (2002) 137-43. Khan's meticulous "appreciation" of Amor at the banquet of Aeneid 1 embodies sophisticated responses to Vergil's presentation of the process through which Dido succumbs to love through the agency of Amor. Five passages of crucial importance are involved: 1.657-60; 673-75; 683-88; 707-14; and 717-22. The analysis and discussion throughout is sensible and sensitive. Tragedy (particularly Aeschylean) and epic are brought into a single focus by several scholars (Krummen, Laigneau, and Scafoglio). Research into the tragic factor in Vergil's epic has become something of a preoccupation of British and North American scholarship and important studies on a major scale are in gestation. Book 5, a perennial favorite of students who are devotees of boating and boxing and contests of a wider variety, gains fresh attention for its larger political implications and its paradigms. Khan, in his study of Aeneid 2.780-84, and 3.1-8, stresses Creusa' s valedictory as prophecy incorporating the fatality and kingdom motifs exploited later in Apollo's oracle, in Anchises' visionary offering, and elsewhere. After arguing that Vergil modelled Charon's challenge to Aeneas in Aeneid 6.388-97 on Aeacus' challenge to Heracles in an extant fragment of Pirithous, composed by Euripides or Critias [CQ 50 (2000) 192-96], Clark now addresses Vergil's re-arrangement and expansion of the Underworld prior to the crossing of the Styx. Aeneas passes through more traditional experiences and horror earlier in his Catabasis than any of his prototypes, Heracles, Dionysus, or Orpheus. Jacobson reads Aeneid 6.45-48 as a question complementing the interrogation Vergilian Bibliography 2001-2002 141 in lines 42ff. Staley's article accents highlights Daedalus' narrative art as explanatory: Exile in Flight; Two Doors, Two Worlds; Cru Love; Inextricabilis Error; Lost Navigators; Fleeing Images; an Fathers and Sons.

Khan's meticulous "appreciation" of Amor at the banquet of Aeneid 1 embodies sophisticated responses to Vergil's presentation of the process through which Dido succumbs to love through the agency of Amor. Five passages of crucial importance are involved: 1.657-60; 673-75; 683-88; 707-14; and 717-22. The analysis and discussion throughout is sensible and sensitive. Tragedy (particularly Aeschylean) and epic are brought into a single focus by several scholars (Krummen, Laigneau, and Scafoglio). Research into the tragic factor in Vergil's epic has become something of a preoccupation of British and North American scholarship and important studies on a major scale are in gestation. Book 5, a perennial favorite of students who are devotees of boating and boxing and contests of a wider variety, gains fresh attention for its larger political implications and its paradigms. Khan, in his study of Aeneid 2.780-84, and 3.1-8, stresses Creusa' s valedictory as prophecy incorporating the fatality and kingdom motifs exploited later in Apollo's oracle, in Anchises' visionary offering, and elsewhere. HorsfalPs somewhat daunting but immensely rich commentary on the Seventh Aeneid continues to excite admiration, respectful queries, and alternative readings. Miller's examination of the Palatine Temple of Apollo as the unexpected goal for Octavian' s victory parade in 23 B.C.E., duly reflected in the central circle of Aeneas' shield, was also favored by Nero in December a.D. 67. Quint's impressive article argues that the memory of Sarpedon, introduced first at Aeneid 1.94-101, with Hector, "dictated the logic of Virgil's imitations of Homer in Book 10." Quint's article submits Book 10 to a searching and skilful analysis and discussion. His concerns are with (1) Nymphs, Ships, and Pines; (2) The Brothers of Sarpedon; (3) The Sequence of Battle in Aeneid 10, concentrating on the principal actors - Pallas, Aeneas, Turnus, Mezentius and Orodes, Mezentius, Lausus, and Aeneas, and their debt to Homeric paradigms; and (4) A Question of Survival. Finally, "Like Pallas, all the Sarpedon-like victims of the Aeneid are dying - in the historical long run - for 1 42 Alexander G . McKay the sake of lulus and a specifically Augustan future lege. raw_text: Sarah Spence. "Pallas/ Athena in and out of the Aeneid." In Athena in the Classical World. Susan Deacy and Alexandra Villing, eds. Leiden, Boston, Köln. 2001. 331-47. Vergilian Bibliography 2001-2002 143 Sarpedon draws the limelight in Aeneid 10 but the cast is also impressive elsewhere. Bateman properly detects Cleopatra and Anton as models for the Punic romance. Christoph parades the Horatii wit Aeneas as heroic examples. Cucchiarelli finds that Ovid's depiction of Orpheus is a mixture of Vergil's Orpheus and of Gallus ( Eclogu 10). Their destinies were closely comparable. Henderson's bree article speculates on the particular case which the Aeneid holds up for our collusion in handling the downside of the creation of a soc memory for Roman Italy. Ovid, Vergil, and Livy, and Ardea, myth cal and actual, are brought into unexpected and mutually informa tive focus; Servius supplies a surprising amen after a recital of su pression and horror at holy Ardea: totius autem Italiae curiosissimum fuisse Vergiliům multifariam apparet. Henderson's parting shot is unsettling and memorable: "The Aeneid winds up - Ovid saw it could run this way - trying harder than ever to make us remember all the forgetting" (p. 23). Read this and wonder. Hughes studies connecting theme and variations involving Creusa and Andromache. Keith's admirably compact volume gains strength in the parade of perceptive reviewers. Panoussi provides a remarkably sensitive perception of Vergil's Ajax from the standpoint of allusion, tragic and heroic identity. Her study joins forces with other scholars who have been recently sensitive to the tragic force of the epic. Perotti' s examination of the virtues and faults of Euryalus and Nisus, particularly in the night exploit, is concise and persuasive. Rivoltella' s essay focuses on the plaintive pursuit of the deceased (Creusa' s retention and elevation is equated with death) by the abandoned (or deceived) male party. Spence's review of the appearances of Athena/Minerva inside and outside the epic action, even by suggestion in the final compact on Olympus, is welcome and provocative.

raw_text: Annie Loupiac. Virgile, Auguste et Apollon: mythes e politique à Rome: L 'Arc et la Lyre. Paris 1999. Rev: Brisson Latomus 60 (1999) 1016-17. 144 Alexander G. McKay Delcourt's review of the role of Evander in the Pallanteum-Rome legend deals effectively with several interpretations of the legend, especially with Vergil, Aeneid 8.844-49. Evander earns respect as a wise king and civilizer, as a veritable humanist before literature itself. His Italian Arcady abides because it embodies the ideal life of humility and proximity to nature. Lest we forget, Dingel' s article highlights Vergil's elevation of the eponym of the Gens Iulia to the stature of king by identifying lulus with Ascanius, but does not make him forefather of the Kings at Alba Longa. Jupiter confirms and endorses the derivation of lulus from Ilus {Aeneid 1 .267 ff.) and so underscores Trojan ancestry.

raw_text: R. F. Thomas. Virgil and the Augustan Rec 2001. Rev: Hardie, TLS 15 June 2001, 32; Hill, G&R 49 (2002) 1 14; Rea, CO 79 (2002) 161; McGill, BMCR 2001. 08. 23. Putnam's contribution to the University of Pennsylvania Conference (#5) detects a distillation of Vergilian themes, ideas, and motifs in Ovid's Myrrha episode, and employs intertextual relationships to explicate the transformation of Vergilian into Ovidian poetry. Ovid's intertextual exploration of Vergilian ideas and motifs sheds light on Aeneas who undergoes 'metamorphosis' into Pallas; he argues that a series of Vergilian transformations through exile and death anticipate the Metamorphoses, Tristia, and Epistulae ex Ponto.
